facts.text: The Great Wall of China took over 2,000 years to build, with construction beginning in the 7th century BC and continuing through the Ming Dynasty.
Cleopatra VII was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt, and she spoke nine languages fluently.
Julius Caesar was assassinated by a group of Roman senators on the Ides of March.
The Viking Age lasted from approximately 793 to 1066 AD, during which Norse seafarers explored, raided, and traded across Europe.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440, revolutionizing the spread of knowledge across Europe.
The ancient city of Pompeii was buried under volcanic ash when Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD, preserving it for nearly 2,000 years.
Napoleon Bonaparte stood about 5 feet 7 inches tall, which was average height for his era — the myth of his shortness came from British propaganda.
The Rosetta Stone, discovered in 1799, was the key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphics because it contained the same text in three scripts.
The Black Death killed an estimated 75–200 million people in Eurasia during the 14th century, wiping out roughly one-third of Europe's population.
The Declaration of Independence was adopted by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776.
Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because it contains ammonia, which acts as a natural cleaning agent.
The Aztec Empire's capital, Tenochtitlan, was built on an island in Lake Texcoco and had a population of over 200,000 at its peak.
Queen Victoria ruled the British Empire for 63 years, from 1837 to 1901, the second-longest reign of any British monarch.
The ancient Library of Alexandria in Egypt was one of the largest and most significant libraries of the ancient world, housing hundreds of thousands of scrolls.
D-Day, the Allied invasion of Normandy, took place on June 6, 1944, and was the largest seaborne invasion in history.
The Indus Valley Civilization, one of the world's oldest, had advanced urban planning with grid-pattern streets and sophisticated drainage systems as early as 2600 BC.
The Magna Carta, signed in 1215, was one of the first documents to limit the power of a king and establish the principle that everyone is subject to the law.
Genghis Khan's Mongol Empire became the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from Eastern Europe to the Sea of Japan.
The Titanic sank on its maiden voyage after hitting an iceberg on April 15, 1912, killing over 1,500 people.
The ancient Greeks held the first Olympic Games in 776 BC at Olympia, and athletes competed in the nude.
Abraham Lincoln was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865.
The Hanging Gardens of Babylon, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, may never have actually existed — no definitive archaeological evidence has been found.
The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille and led to the end of the French monarchy.
Magellan's expedition was the first to circumnavigate the globe, though Magellan himself was killed in the Philippines before completing the journey.
The Cuban Missile Crisis in October 1962 brought the United States and Soviet Union to the brink of nuclear war for 13 tense days.
Ancient Egyptians mummified not only humans but also cats, dogs, birds, and even crocodiles as offerings to the gods.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War and the reunification of Germany.
The Inca Empire used a system of knotted strings called quipu for record-keeping instead of a written language.
Winston Churchill led Britain through World War II and is famous for his rousing speeches that boosted British morale during the Blitz.
The Byzantine Empire, the eastern half of the Roman Empire, lasted for over 1,000 years after the fall of Rome in 476 AD.
The Wright Brothers made the first successful powered airplane flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, on December 17, 1903.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
The Crusades were a series of religious wars between Christians and Muslims fought primarily for control of the Holy Land from 1096 to 1291.
The Mayan civilization developed one of the most advanced writing systems in the pre-Columbian Americas and created a remarkably accurate calendar.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria on June 28, 1914, triggered the chain of events that led to World War I.
During the California Gold Rush of 1849, the population of San Francisco grew from about 1,000 to 25,000 in just two years.
The ancient Roman Colosseum could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and was used for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
The Treaty of Versailles, signed in 1919, officially ended World War I but imposed harsh penalties on Germany that contributed to World War II.
Leonardo da Vinci wrote his notes in mirror script, from right to left, possibly to keep his ideas secret or simply because he was left-handed.
The Romanov dynasty ruled Russia for over 300 years before being overthrown in the Russian Revolution of 1917.
Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, brought the United States into World War II.
In ancient Sparta, boys were taken from their families at age seven to begin rigorous military training known as the agoge.
The Suez Canal, opened in 1869, connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea and dramatically shortened shipping routes between Europe and Asia.
Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire — teaching existed at Oxford as early as 1096, while the Aztec civilization began around 1325.
The Spanish Inquisition, established in 1478, was a tribunal set up to enforce Catholic orthodoxy in Spain.
Alexander the Great created one of the largest empires in ancient history by the age of 30, stretching from Greece to northwestern India.
The Emancipation Proclamation, issued by Abraham Lincoln on January 1, 1863, declared freedom for enslaved people in Confederate states.
The Space Race between the US and Soviet Union was a key part of the Cold War, culminating in the Apollo 11 moon landing in 1969.
Ancient Mesopotamia, located in modern-day Iraq, is often called the 'Cradle of Civilization' because it was home to some of the earliest known civilizations.
Turkeys were once worshipped as gods by the Mayan people, who believed they were vessels of the gods.
The Hundred Years' War between England and France actually lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453.
The first telephone call was made by Alexander Graham Bell on March 10, 1876, when he said 'Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you.'
The Holocaust was the systematic murder of six million Jews by the Nazi regime during World War II.
The Terracotta Army, discovered in 1974, consists of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers built to protect China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang, in the afterlife.
Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his famous 'I Have a Dream' speech during the March on Washington on August 28, 1963.
Vikings used sunstones — a type of calcite crystal — to navigate the open ocean even on cloudy days.
The ancient city of Petra in Jordan was carved directly into rose-colored rock faces by the Nabataeans over 2,000 years ago.
The US Constitution, ratified in 1788, is the oldest written national constitution still in use today.
Catherine the Great of Russia expanded the Russian Empire significantly and was one of the most powerful women in history.
The atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, killing an estimated 80,000 people instantly.
The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, facilitating the exchange of goods, ideas, and cultures.
In medieval England, animals could be put on trial and even sentenced to death for crimes such as murder.
The Panama Canal, completed in 1914, is a 50-mile waterway that connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
Galileo Galilei was placed under house arrest by the Catholic Church in 1633 for supporting the heliocentric model of the solar system.
The Battle of Gettysburg in July 1863 was a turning point in the American Civil War and the bloodiest battle ever fought on American soil.
The ancient Egyptian pharaoh Hatshepsut was one of the most successful female rulers in history, reigning for over 20 years.
Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
The Great Fire of London in 1666 destroyed much of the medieval city but also helped end the Great Plague by killing the rats that carried it.
The Ottoman Empire lasted over 600 years, from 1299 to 1922, and at its height controlled much of southeastern Europe, western Asia, and North Africa.
The Pony Express, a mail delivery service in the American Wild West, only operated for about 18 months before being replaced by the telegraph.
The Trojan War, described in Homer's Iliad, may have been based on a real conflict between the Greeks and the city of Troy around 1200 BC.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, on October 4, 1957, marking the start of the Space Age.
In ancient Rome, gladiators rarely fought to the death — they were expensive to train and maintain, so matches were usually carefully managed.
The Protestant Reformation began in 1517 when Martin Luther nailed his Ninety-Five Theses to the door of a church in Wittenberg, Germany.
Machu Picchu, the Incan citadel in Peru, was built around 1450 and was never discovered by Spanish conquistadors.
The Battle of Thermopylae in 480 BC saw 300 Spartans and their allies make a legendary last stand against a massive Persian army.
The first successful vaccine was developed by Edward Jenner in 1796 to prevent smallpox.
The Berlin Wall stood for 28 years, from 1961 to 1989, dividing East and West Berlin during the Cold War.
Ancient Egyptian workers who built the pyramids were not slaves but paid laborers who received beer as part of their daily rations.
Christopher Columbus reached the Americas on October 12, 1492, though he believed he had reached Asia.
The samurai were the warrior class of feudal Japan, following a strict code of honor called bushido.
The Industrial Revolution, beginning in the late 18th century, transformed economies from agrarian to industrial and began in Great Britain.
King Henry VIII of England had six wives and broke with the Catholic Church to establish the Church of England so he could annul his first marriage.
The Boston Tea Party in 1773 was a political protest by American colonists against British taxation without representation.
The Trans-Siberian Railway, completed in 1916, is the longest rail line in the world, stretching 5,772 miles across Russia.
The Zulu Kingdom under King Shaka in the early 19th century revolutionized warfare in southern Africa with innovative military tactics.
The construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza took approximately 20 years and required an estimated 2.3 million stone blocks.
The War of the Roses was a series of English civil wars fought between the houses of Lancaster and York from 1455 to 1487.
The first manned Moon landing occurred during Apollo 11 in 1969, fulfilling President Kennedy's goal set earlier that decade.
The Code of Hammurabi, dating to about 1754 BC, is one of the oldest deciphered writings of significant length and established 282 laws.
Albert Einstein published his theory of special relativity in 1905, fundamentally changing our understanding of space and time.
The Bastille, a medieval fortress in Paris, was stormed on July 14, 1789, marking the beginning of the French Revolution.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition from 1804 to 1806 explored the western territory of the United States all the way to the Pacific Ocean.
The Parthenon in Athens was built between 447 and 432 BC as a temple dedicated to the goddess Athena.
The Korean War, fought from 1950 to 1953, ended in an armistice but technically never ended with a peace treaty.
During the height of the Roman Empire, a network of roads spanning over 250,000 miles connected the vast territory — many are still in use today.
The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was one of the first major books produced using movable type in Europe.
The ancient Chinese invented gunpowder during the 9th century, originally using it for fireworks and later for weapons.
The sinking of the Lusitania in 1915 by a German U-boat helped turn public opinion and eventually brought the United States into World War I.
The Hagia Sophia in Istanbul served as a Christian cathedral, a mosque, and is now a museum that has stood for nearly 1,500 years.
The September 11 attacks in 2001 resulted in the deaths of nearly 3,000 people and fundamentally changed US foreign policy.
The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar on August 27, 1896, lasting only 38 to 45 minutes.
Feudalism was the dominant social system in medieval Europe, in which lords granted land to vassals in exchange for military service.
The ancient Phoenicians, based in modern-day Lebanon, created one of the first alphabets, which influenced Greek and Latin scripts.
The Gettysburg Address, delivered by Abraham Lincoln in November 1863, is considered one of the most important speeches in American history.
The discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming in 1928 revolutionized medicine and saved millions of lives.
The Great Depression began with the stock market crash on October 29, 1929, and lasted until the late 1930s.
Genghis Khan was responsible for the deaths of an estimated 40 million people, approximately 10% of the world's population at the time.
Joan of Arc, a teenage peasant girl, led the French army to several victories during the Hundred Years' War before being burned at the stake in 1431.
The Suez Crisis of 1956 marked the end of Britain and France's era as major world powers and the rise of American and Soviet influence.
The Mayflower carried the Pilgrims from England to Plymouth, Massachusetts, in 1620, establishing one of the first permanent European settlements in North America.
Trench warfare defined much of World War I, with soldiers living in muddy, disease-ridden trenches for months at a time.
The ancient city of Babylon, in modern-day Iraq, was one of the most important cities in the ancient world and home to the legendary Hanging Gardens.
In 1835, the US national debt was zero — the only time in history the country was completely debt-free.
The Meiji Restoration in 1868 transformed Japan from a feudal society into a modern industrialized nation within a few decades.
The Trail of Tears in the 1830s was the forced relocation of Native American peoples from their homelands to territory west of the Mississippi River.
The Sphinx of Giza is believed to be over 4,500 years old, making it one of the oldest and largest sculptures in the world.
The V-E Day celebrations on May 8, 1945, marked the end of World War II in Europe.
The Persian Empire under Cyrus the Great practiced religious tolerance and is sometimes called the first human rights champion.
Nicolaus Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model of the solar system in 1543, suggesting that Earth revolves around the Sun rather than the other way around.
The ancient Olympic Games were held every four years for nearly 12 centuries, from 776 BC to 393 AD.
The Battle of Waterloo in 1815 ended Napoleon's rule as Emperor of France and reshaped the political map of Europe.
The Domesday Book, commissioned by William the Conqueror in 1086, was a comprehensive survey of England's landholdings and resources.
The discovery of the New World by Columbus in 1492 initiated a period of European exploration, colonization, and exploitation of the Americas.
The first atomic bomb test, code-named Trinity, took place on July 16, 1945, in New Mexico.
The Khmer Empire built the vast temple complex of Angkor Wat in Cambodia in the 12th century, the largest religious monument in the world.
During the Viking Age, Leif Erikson reached North America roughly 500 years before Columbus.
The Charge of the Light Brigade during the Crimean War in 1854 became famous for its tragic futility as British cavalry charged directly into Russian cannon fire.
The construction of Stonehenge in England began around 3000 BC, and its exact purpose remains a mystery.
The Cuban Revolution, led by Fidel Castro, overthrew the Batista regime in 1959 and established a communist government in Cuba.
The Transcontinental Railroad was completed on May 10, 1869, connecting the east and west coasts of the United States.
The Pharos of Alexandria, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, was a lighthouse that stood over 300 feet tall.
The Battle of Hastings in 1066 changed the course of English history when William the Conqueror defeated King Harold II.
Marie Antoinette never actually said 'Let them eat cake' — the phrase was attributed to her by political opponents.
The Boer Wars in South Africa between the British Empire and the Boer states introduced concentration camps, where thousands of civilians died.
The Marshall Plan, implemented after World War II, provided over $12 billion in economic aid to help rebuild European economies.
The Colossus of Rhodes, a giant statue of the sun god Helios, stood at the entrance to the harbor of Rhodes circa 280 BC.
The Women's Suffrage Movement in the United States achieved its goal when the 19th Amendment was ratified on August 18, 1920.
The Han Dynasty in China lasted over 400 years and is considered a golden age in Chinese history.
Thomas Edison patented the phonograph in 1878, making it possible to record and play back sound for the first time.
The guillotine was actually invented as a more humane method of execution and was used in France until 1977.
The Spanish Armada, a fleet of 130 ships sent by Spain to invade England in 1588, was defeated by the English navy and severe weather.
The Nuremberg Trials after World War II were the first international war crimes tribunals, prosecuting prominent leaders of Nazi Germany.
The ancient Sumerians of Mesopotamia invented the wheel around 3500 BC, initially using it for pottery rather than transportation.
The fall of Constantinople to the Ottoman Turks in 1453 marked the end of the Byzantine Empire and the beginning of Ottoman dominance in the region.
The United Nations was established on October 24, 1945, to promote international cooperation and prevent future world wars.
Ancient Romans had a festival called Saturnalia where masters served their slaves, and social norms were reversed for a week.
The Reconquista was a 780-year effort by Christian kingdoms to recapture the Iberian Peninsula from the Moors, ending in 1492.
The Opium Wars between Britain and China in the mid-19th century led to the ceding of Hong Kong to Britain.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered by French soldiers in Egypt in 1799 and became the key to understanding ancient Egyptian writing.
Marco Polo traveled from Venice to China in the 13th century and spent 24 years in Asia, documenting his experiences in a famous book.
The Battle of Stalingrad, fought from 1942 to 1943, is considered the bloodiest battle in history with nearly 2 million casualties.
President Andrew Jackson once held an open house party at the White House where a 1,400-pound block of cheese was served to guests.
The Boxer Rebellion in China at the turn of the 20th century was an anti-foreign uprising that was suppressed by an international coalition.
The Erie Canal, completed in 1825, connected the Great Lakes to the Hudson River and helped make New York City the commercial capital of the United States.
The English Civil War in the 1640s led to the execution of King Charles I and the establishment of a republic under Oliver Cromwell.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was completed in 1858, allowing messages to be sent between North America and Europe in minutes instead of weeks.
The Armistice that ended World War I was signed on November 11, 1918, at the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month.
Cleopatra was actually Greek by heritage, not Egyptian — she was a descendant of Ptolemy I, one of Alexander the Great's generals.
The Mughal Empire ruled much of the Indian subcontinent from 1526 to 1857 and built architectural marvels like the Taj Mahal.
The Louisiana Purchase in 1803 doubled the size of the United States, acquiring approximately 828,000 square miles from France for $15 million.
The Enlightenment was an intellectual movement in 18th-century Europe that emphasized reason, science, and individual rights.
The Bay of Pigs invasion in April 1961 was a failed attempt by the CIA to overthrow Fidel Castro's government in Cuba.
The ancient city of Mohenjo-daro in the Indus Valley had a sophisticated sewage and drainage system over 4,000 years ago.
The Tower of London has served as a royal palace, prison, armory, treasury, and zoo throughout its over 900-year history.
The Crimean War of 1853–1856 was notable for Florence Nightingale's pioneering work in modern nursing.
The Renaissance began in Italy in the 14th century and spread across Europe, marking a cultural rebirth of art, science, and learning.
The Battle of Midway in June 1942 was a decisive naval battle in the Pacific Theater that turned the tide of World War II against Japan.
The Cherokee Nation had its own written language, created by Sequoyah in 1821, making it one of the first Native American peoples with a writing system.
The Dreyfus Affair in France in the 1890s exposed deep-seated antisemitism and divided French society.
The last execution by guillotine in France occurred on September 10, 1977 — the same year Star Wars was released.
The Peloponnesian War between Athens and Sparta lasted 27 years and ultimately weakened the Greek city-states.
The first subway system in the world opened in London in 1863, known as the Metropolitan Railway.
The Bolshevik Revolution in October 1917 overthrew the Russian Provisional Government and led to the creation of the Soviet Union.
The ancient Egyptians used a 365-day calendar, one of the first to closely match the solar year.
The Montgomery Bus Boycott began on December 5, 1955, after Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat, lasting 381 days.
The Carthaginian general Hannibal famously crossed the Alps with war elephants to attack Rome in 218 BC.
In 1932, Australia fought the 'Great Emu War' against a population of emus — and lost, failing to significantly reduce their numbers.
The Forbidden City in Beijing was the imperial palace of China for nearly 500 years and contains 980 buildings.
The Balfour Declaration of 1917 expressed British support for the establishment of a Jewish homeland in Palestine.
The ancient Greek physician Hippocrates is considered the 'Father of Medicine' and established medicine as a profession separate from religion.
The Treaty of Tordesillas in 1494 divided the newly discovered lands of the Americas between Spain and Portugal.
VJ Day, marking the end of World War II in the Pacific, was celebrated on August 15, 1945.
The Aztecs sacrificed an estimated 20,000 people per year to their gods, believing it necessary to keep the sun moving across the sky.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 89% of its value during the Great Depression, dropping from 381 to 41 between 1929 and 1932.
The Magna Carta was issued by King John of England at Runnymede in 1215, establishing principles still reflected in modern constitutional law.
The Scramble for Africa in the late 19th century saw European powers carve up the African continent, controlling approximately 90% of it by 1914.
The ancient Persians invented the postal system around 550 BC under the reign of Cyrus the Great.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1986, and remains the worst nuclear power plant accident in history.
The Salem Witch Trials of 1692 resulted in the execution of 20 people accused of witchcraft in colonial Massachusetts.
The Easter Island statues, called moai, were carved by the Rapa Nui people between 1250 and 1500 AD, and some weigh over 80 tons.
Ancient Sparta had two kings who ruled simultaneously, a unique arrangement in the ancient world.
The Erie Railroad War of 1868 was a battle between Jay Gould, Jim Fisk, and Cornelius Vanderbilt for control of the Erie Railroad.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space in 2012.
The Battle of Marathon in 490 BC saw the Athenians defeat a much larger Persian force, preserving Greek independence.
The Rwandan Genocide in 1994 resulted in the deaths of an estimated 800,000 people in approximately 100 days.
The Colossus of Rhodes stood for only 54 years before being destroyed by an earthquake in 226 BC.
Abraham Lincoln, who championed the abolition of slavery, once lost a Senate race to Stephen Douglas in 1858 before winning the presidency in 1860.
The first permanent English settlement in the Americas was established at Jamestown, Virginia, in 1607.
The Taiping Rebellion in China (1850-1864) was one of the deadliest conflicts in history, with an estimated 20 to 30 million deaths.
The Eiffel Tower was built as a temporary structure for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris and was almost demolished in 1909.
The Apollo 13 mission in April 1970 survived a catastrophic oxygen tank explosion and safely returned its crew to Earth.
The first known use of biological warfare occurred in 1346 when the Mongols catapulted plague-infected corpses into the besieged city of Caffa.
The Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 was the first major domestic challenge to the authority of the new US federal government.
The Zimmermann Telegram of 1917, in which Germany proposed a military alliance with Mexico against the US, helped draw America into World War I.
The Roman Republic transitioned to the Roman Empire in 27 BC when Octavian became Augustus, the first Roman emperor.
The Indian independence movement, led by Mahatma Gandhi, achieved independence from British rule on August 15, 1947.
The discovery of King Tutankhamun's nearly intact tomb by Howard Carter in 1922 was one of the greatest archaeological finds in history.
The ancient Greeks had a word, 'ostracism,' from which we get our word 'ostracize' — citizens could vote to banish someone for 10 years by writing their name on a potsherd.
The first successful powered flight by the Wright Brothers lasted only 12 seconds and covered just 120 feet.
The Russo-Japanese War of 1904-1905 was the first modern war in which an Asian country defeated a European power.
The Palace of Versailles was originally a hunting lodge before being expanded by Louis XIV into one of the most opulent palaces in history.
The Battle of the Bulge in December 1944 was the last major German offensive of World War II on the Western Front.
Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 27 years before becoming South Africa's first Black president in 1994.
The ancient Roman aqueduct system brought fresh water over distances of up to 60 miles, supplying cities with millions of gallons daily.
The Alamo in Texas was the site of a famous 1836 battle during the Texas Revolution against Mexico.
The Peace of Westphalia in 1648 ended the Thirty Years' War and established the modern concept of national sovereignty.
The first Olympic Games of the modern era were held in Athens, Greece, in 1896.
The Manhattan Project was the secret US program that developed the first nuclear weapons during World War II.
The ancient Chinese began building the Grand Canal around 486 BC, making it the longest artificial waterway in the world at over 1,100 miles.
Napoleon was once attacked by a horde of rabbits during a hunting party — the rabbits had been domesticated and charged at him expecting food.
The storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789, is celebrated as France's national holiday, Bastille Day.
The Inca Empire stretched over 2,500 miles along the western coast of South America and had a road system spanning about 25,000 miles.
The Texas Revolution of 1835-1836 resulted in Texas gaining independence from Mexico before later joining the United States.
The first Geneva Convention was adopted in 1864, establishing rules for the humane treatment of wounded soldiers.
The Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 was the first significant law restricting immigration into the United States.
Tsutomu Yamaguchi survived both atomic bombings — he was in Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, and returned to his home in Nagasaki just in time for the second bombing on August 9.
The Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s and 1930s was a cultural, social, and artistic explosion centered in Harlem, New York.
The Punic Wars between Rome and Carthage lasted over 100 years and ended with the complete destruction of Carthage in 146 BC.
The Panama Canal took 10 years to build and cost over 25,000 workers their lives due to disease and landslides.
The Velvet Revolution in Czechoslovakia in 1989 peacefully ended communist rule in the country within six weeks.
The invention of the compass by the Chinese during the Han Dynasty revolutionized navigation and exploration.
The ancient Maya practiced skull modification on infants, binding boards to babies' heads to create elongated skulls as a sign of beauty.
The War of 1812 between the US and Britain included the burning of Washington, D.C., and the writing of 'The Star-Spangled Banner.'
The Berlin Conference of 1884-1885 regulated European colonization and trade in Africa, leading to the Scramble for Africa.
The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered between 1947 and 1956, are among the oldest known manuscripts of the Hebrew Bible.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition only had one fatality — Sergeant Charles Floyd, who likely died from a burst appendix.
The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire on March 25, 1911, killed 146 workers and led to major reforms in US labor laws.
In medieval times, bread was used as a plate — stale slices called 'trenchers' held food and were eaten or given to the poor afterward.
The Stamp Act of 1765 was one of the key grievances that led to the American Revolution, as colonists protested taxation without representation.
The Acropolis of Athens has been standing for over 2,500 years and was the center of ancient Athenian life.
The Yalta Conference in February 1945 was a meeting of Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin to plan the postwar reorganization of Europe.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Dr. Christiaan Barnard on December 3, 1967, in Cape Town, South Africa.
The Aztec practice of human sacrifice was tied to their belief that the sun god required human blood to continue his journey across the sky.
The Napoleonic Code, introduced in 1804, influenced legal systems worldwide and established principles like the presumption of innocence.
The Colosseum in Rome was built with a retractable awning system called the velarium to shade spectators from the sun.
The Meiji Constitution of 1889 was the first modern constitution in Asia and established Japan as a constitutional monarchy.
The Battle of Agincourt in 1415 was a major English victory during the Hundred Years' War, where English longbowmen devastated the French army.
The first commercial radio broadcast took place on November 2, 1920, from station KDKA in Pittsburgh, reporting election results.
The Ming Dynasty in China (1368-1644) oversaw the construction of the Forbidden City, the expansion of the Great Wall, and the voyages of Admiral Zheng He.
The Teapot Dome Scandal in the 1920s was one of the most significant political corruption scandals in US history before Watergate.
Queen Elizabeth I of England never married and was known as the 'Virgin Queen,' after whom the colony of Virginia was named.
The Franco-Prussian War of 1870-1871 led to the unification of Germany under Otto von Bismarck.
The ancient Greeks invented democracy in Athens around 508 BC, though only free adult males could participate.
The Battle of Gallipoli in 1915 was a failed Allied campaign to open a sea route to Russia through the Ottoman Empire.
The Missouri Compromise of 1820 temporarily resolved the issue of slavery's expansion in the United States by admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state.
The fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975, marked the end of the Vietnam War and the reunification of Vietnam under communist rule.
Ancient Roman concrete was stronger than modern concrete — structures like the Pantheon have lasted over 2,000 years without reinforcement.
The Spanish Civil War from 1936 to 1939 was a precursor to World War II and saw the rise of Francisco Franco's dictatorship.
The Haymarket Affair of 1886 in Chicago was a pivotal event in the history of the labor movement and the fight for the eight-hour workday.
The Parthenon's columns are slightly curved and tilted inward to create an optical illusion of perfect straightness.
The Watergate scandal led to the resignation of President Richard Nixon on August 9, 1974, the only US president to resign.
The Song Dynasty in China (960-1279) saw the invention of movable type printing, the compass, and gunpowder weapons.
George Washington was unanimously elected as the first President of the United States and voluntarily stepped down after two terms.
The Bayeux Tapestry, actually an embroidery, is a 230-foot-long cloth depicting the events leading up to the Norman conquest of England in 1066.
The assassination of President John F. Kennedy on November 22, 1963, in Dallas, Texas, shocked the world.
The Iron Curtain was a political boundary dividing Europe into two separate areas during the Cold War.
The ancient city of Carthage, located in modern-day Tunisia, was a major power in the Mediterranean before being destroyed by Rome.
The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971, and he chose the '@' symbol to separate the user name from the computer name.
The Dust Bowl of the 1930s devastated the Great Plains of the United States, displacing hundreds of thousands of families.
The Spanish flu pandemic of 1918-1919 infected about one-third of the world's population and killed an estimated 50 million people.
Alexander the Great named over 70 cities after himself, including Alexandria in Egypt, but also one after his horse, Bucephalus.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England replaced King James II with William and Mary without major bloodshed.
The construction of the Transcontinental Railroad relied heavily on the labor of Chinese immigrants and Irish workers.
The ancient Romans built heated floors called hypocausts, an early form of central heating used in baths and wealthy homes.
The Potsdam Conference in 1945 established the framework for postwar Germany, dividing it into four occupation zones.
The first successful solo nonstop transatlantic flight was made by Charles Lindbergh on May 20-21, 1927.
The Rosetta Stone contains a decree issued in 196 BC on behalf of King Ptolemy V, written in three scripts: hieroglyphic, demotic, and Greek.
During World War II, a bear named Wojtek was officially enlisted in the Polish army and helped carry ammunition at the Battle of Monte Cassino.
The abolitionist movement in the 19th century fought to end slavery in the United States and the British Empire.
The Great Schism of 1054 permanently divided Christianity into the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church.
The Battle of Lepanto in 1571 was a decisive naval battle that halted Ottoman expansion into the western Mediterranean.
The Nuremberg Laws of 1935 stripped Jewish people of German citizenship and prohibited marriages between Jews and non-Jews.
The ancient city of Troy, long thought to be mythical, was discovered by archaeologist Heinrich Schliemann in the 1870s.
The Antarctic Treaty, signed on December 1, 1959, dedicated the continent to peaceful scientific research and banned military activity.
Ancient Egyptian pharaohs kept their hair covered at all times and were never seen without their royal headdress, called a nemes.
The Hundred Days was the period in 1815 when Napoleon escaped from exile on Elba and briefly returned to power before his defeat at Waterloo.
The Gutenberg Revolution, enabled by the printing press, is considered one of the most important events in human history for spreading literacy and knowledge.
The Gold Rush of 1848-1855 in California attracted over 300,000 people from around the world seeking fortune.
The Camp David Accords, signed in 1978, were a landmark peace agreement between Egypt and Israel, brokered by President Jimmy Carter.
The first use of tanks in warfare occurred during the Battle of the Somme in September 1916 in World War I.
The Incas performed successful skull surgeries called trepanation, and many patients survived, as evidenced by healed bone growth.
The Marshall Plan helped rebuild Western Europe after World War II and is credited with preventing the spread of communism in those countries.
The Dred Scott decision of 1857 ruled that African Americans could not be US citizens, inflaming tensions that led to the Civil War.
The ancient Minoans on Crete built the Palace of Knossos around 1700 BC, which may have inspired the myth of the Labyrinth.
The first shots of the American Revolution were fired at Lexington and Concord on April 19, 1775.
The Trans-Atlantic slave trade forcibly transported an estimated 12.5 million Africans to the Americas between the 16th and 19th centuries.
In 1752, Britain adopted the Gregorian calendar and 'lost' 11 days — September 2 was followed by September 14, causing widespread confusion.
The Opium Wars led to the Treaty of Nanking in 1842, which ceded Hong Kong to Britain and opened five Chinese ports to foreign trade.
The Great Zimbabwe ruins in southern Africa were the center of a thriving trading empire between the 11th and 15th centuries.
The Manhattan Project employed over 125,000 workers, yet many had no idea they were helping build an atomic bomb.
The Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses and brought the Tudor dynasty to power in England.
The first telephone exchange opened in New Haven, Connecticut, in 1878, with just 21 subscribers.
The original purpose of the Leaning Tower of Pisa was as a bell tower for the cathedral, and it began leaning during construction in 1173 due to soft ground.
The Seneca Falls Convention of 1848 was the first women's rights convention in the United States, marking the beginning of the organized suffrage movement.
The Qin Dynasty, though lasting only 15 years (221-206 BC), unified China, standardized weights and measures, and began construction of the Great Wall.
The Stonewall riots of June 1969 in New York City are considered a catalyst for the modern LGBTQ rights movement.
The Tang Dynasty (618-907) is often considered the golden age of Chinese civilization, known for its art, literature, and cosmopolitan culture.
The first photograph was taken by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce in 1826 or 1827, requiring an exposure time of about eight hours.
The Children's Crusade of 1212 was an ill-fated attempt by thousands of children to march to the Holy Land — most never arrived.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, adopted during the French Revolution in 1789, inspired human rights documents worldwide.
The Transcontinental Railroad was joined at Promontory Summit, Utah, with a golden spike ceremony on May 10, 1869.
The Portuguese Empire was the first global maritime empire, spanning nearly six centuries from the 15th to the 20th century.
The Munich Agreement of 1938 allowed Nazi Germany to annex the Sudetenland, and British PM Neville Chamberlain declared it would bring 'peace for our time.'
The Korean Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) is the most heavily fortified border in the world and has stood since 1953.
During the construction of the Great Wall of China, workers mixed sticky rice into the mortar, making it stronger than modern cement.
The Pilgrims celebrated the first Thanksgiving in 1621 with the Wampanoag people, though the holiday wasn't made official until 1863.
The Persian Wars between Greece and Persia in the 5th century BC helped define Western civilization and democracy.
The Apollo 11 mission's computer had less processing power than a modern pocket calculator.
The Yom Kippur War of 1973 began when Egypt and Syria launched a surprise attack on Israel on the Jewish holy day.
The first electric traffic light was installed in Cleveland, Ohio, on August 5, 1914.
The Great Famine in Ireland (1845-1852) caused by potato blight resulted in the death or emigration of approximately 25% of the population.
The ancient Akkadian Empire, founded by Sargon of Akkad around 2334 BC, is considered the world's first empire.
The Brown v. Board of Education Supreme Court decision on May 17, 1954, declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional.
The ancient Romans added lead to their wine as a sweetener, which likely contributed to widespread lead poisoning.
The Thirty Years' War (1618-1648) devastated central Europe and killed an estimated 8 million people.
The Pax Romana was a 200-year period of relative peace and stability across the Roman Empire, lasting from 27 BC to 180 AD.
The Iranian Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Shah and established an Islamic Republic under Ayatollah Khomeini.
The first World's Fair, the Great Exhibition of 1851, was held in London's Crystal Palace and attracted over 6 million visitors.
The Voting Rights Act was signed into law by President Lyndon B. Johnson on August 6, 1965, prohibiting racial discrimination in voting.
The Wright Brothers' first successful airplane was called the Wright Flyer and cost less than $1,000 to build.
The Maginot Line, France's extensive defensive fortification along the German border, was easily bypassed by German forces who invaded through Belgium in 1940.
The Plymouth Colony, established by the Pilgrims in 1620, was the second successful English settlement in North America.
The Great Leap Forward in China (1958-1962) was a disastrous economic campaign that led to a famine killing an estimated 15 to 55 million people.
The Rosetta Stone is currently housed in the British Museum in London, where it has been since 1802.
The Congress of Vienna in 1814-1815 redrew the map of Europe after the Napoleonic Wars and established a balance of power.
The Tang Dynasty poet Li Bai is said to have drowned while trying to embrace the reflection of the Moon in a river after drinking too much wine.
The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) was the only successful slave revolt that led to the creation of an independent nation, Haiti.
The ancient Egyptians invented toothpaste using a mixture of crushed eggshells, ox hooves, and pumice.
The Tokugawa Shogunate ruled Japan for over 250 years (1603-1868) during a period of relative peace known as the Edo period.
The first Geneva Convention of 1864 was inspired by Henry Dunant's horrific experience at the Battle of Solferino in 1859.
The Pueblo Revolt of 1680 was the most successful Native American uprising against Spanish colonizers in North America.
The ancient city of Ur in Mesopotamia contained a massive ziggurat dedicated to the moon god Nanna, built around 2100 BC.
The British East India Company was once the most powerful corporation in history, with its own army of over 260,000 soldiers.
The Siege of Vienna in 1683 marked the turning point against Ottoman expansion into Europe.
The Code Talkers of World War II were Native Americans who used their indigenous languages to create unbreakable military codes.
The first census in recorded history was conducted in ancient Egypt around 3000 BC to plan the construction of the pyramids.
The Boxer Protocol of 1901 imposed harsh penalties on China and allowed foreign troops to be stationed in Beijing.
The Ptolemaic dynasty of ancient Egypt lasted nearly 300 years, and Cleopatra VII was its final ruler.
During the Middle Ages, European cathedrals took an average of 200 to 300 years to complete.
The Abbasid Caliphate's House of Wisdom in Baghdad was one of the greatest centers of learning in the medieval world.
The Siege of Leningrad during World War II lasted 872 days, making it one of the longest and most destructive sieges in history.
The first successful blood transfusion was performed by Dr. James Blundell in 1818, using human blood.
The Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died during childbirth in 1631.
Benjamin Franklin's famous kite experiment to prove the electrical nature of lightning took place in June 1752.
The Battle of Trafalgar in 1805 established British naval supremacy for over a century, though Admiral Nelson was killed during the battle.
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was built on an island and connected to the mainland by causeways, with a population larger than most European cities of the time.
The ancient Olympic truce, called the ekecheiria, halted all conflicts in Greece so athletes could travel safely to the games.
The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact of 1939 was a non-aggression agreement between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union that shocked the world.
The discovery of the Americas by Europeans triggered the Columbian Exchange, transferring plants, animals, and diseases between the Old and New Worlds.
The Rosie the Riveter campaign during World War II encouraged millions of American women to work in factories and shipyards.
The Sumerians invented cuneiform writing around 3400 BC, one of the earliest known systems of writing.
The USS Maine exploded in Havana Harbor on February 15, 1898, helping trigger the Spanish-American War.
The ancient Egyptians played a board game called Senet, which is one of the oldest known board games, dating back to around 3100 BC.
The Warsaw Pact, formed in 1955, was the Soviet Union's military alliance counterpart to NATO.
The ancient Phoenicians were the first civilization to circumnavigate Africa, accomplishing the feat around 600 BC.
The Second Boer War (1899-1902) saw the British use scorched earth tactics and concentration camps against the Boer civilian population.
The East Berlin uprising on June 17, 1953, was one of the first major rebellions against Soviet control in Eastern Europe.
The Seven Years' War (1756-1763) is sometimes called the first true 'world war' as it was fought across five continents.
The Ottoman Empire's elite soldiers, the Janissaries, were originally enslaved Christian boys converted to Islam and trained from childhood.
The Gregorian calendar, which most of the world uses today, was introduced by Pope Gregory XIII in October 1582.
The ancient Persians had an early form of refrigeration called a yakhchal, an ice house that kept food cold in desert conditions.
The Berlin Airlift of 1948-1949 delivered over 2.3 million tons of supplies to West Berlin during the Soviet blockade.
The first Nobel Prizes were awarded on December 10, 1901, in the fields of physics, chemistry, medicine, literature, and peace.
The Safavid Empire established Shia Islam as the state religion of Persia in the 16th century, shaping Iranian identity to this day.
The Rosetta Stone weighs approximately 1,680 pounds and stands about 3 feet 9 inches tall.
The Boxer Rebellion was triggered partly by resentment of foreign influence and Christian missionary activities in China.
The first Continental Congress met in Philadelphia in September 1774 to coordinate a colonial response to British tyranny.
The ancient Carthaginians were master sailors and traders, establishing colonies across the Mediterranean.
The Long March of 1934-1935 was a 6,000-mile retreat by the Chinese Communist forces that solidified Mao Zedong's leadership.
The Lascaux cave paintings in France are approximately 17,000 years old and depict large animals and human figures.
In ancient Athens, the penalty for stealing vegetables was reportedly more severe than the penalty for murder.
The Sykes-Picot Agreement of 1916 secretly divided the Ottoman Empire's territories between Britain and France, reshaping the Middle East.
The Versailles Treaty required Germany to accept full responsibility for World War I and pay reparations equivalent to $33 billion.
The Age of Enlightenment produced influential thinkers such as Voltaire, John Locke, and Jean-Jacques Rousseau.
The Battle of Iwo Jima in February-March 1945 was one of the bloodiest battles in the Pacific Theater, with nearly 7,000 US Marines killed.
The Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 were pro-democracy demonstrations in Beijing that were violently suppressed by the Chinese government.
